skill.name: hummingbot
skill.description: Hummingbot documentation skill for automated crypto trading, market making, arbitrage, exchange connectors, Gateway setup, and strategy development.
skill.summary: Use Hummingbot documentation to plan, configure, and troubleshoot crypto trading bots.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: The skill is generated Hummingbot documentation, not executable helper code, so many static hits are documentation examples rather than active behavior. However, the references include privileged shell commands, Docker socket permission changes, credential-file deletion, and secret-key configuration examples that create high operational risk if followed without review. No evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Docker Socket Permission Guidance
high_findings.description: The troubleshooting guide recommends adding the user to the docker group and then running chmod 666 on /var/run/docker.sock. This can expose root-equivalent Docker control to any local user and is unsafe marketplace guidance.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command changes Docker socket permissions to world-writable. The context is troubleshooting documentation, not malware, but the security impact is clear.

high_findings.title: Privileged Deletion of Credential Files
high_findings.description: The documentation tells users to run sudo rm -rf on the password verification file and to delete encrypted credential files. The guidance is intended for password reset, but it can destroy local encrypted API-key and private-key material.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly discuss encrypted connector files, API keys, private keys, and privileged deletion. The intent appears administrative, not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Trading Key Configuration Examples
medium_findings.description: The references include API key, API secret, and wallet secret key configuration guidance. This is expected for Hummingbot, but it increases the chance that users expose exchange or wallet credentials in prompts or files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations directly reference API key, API secret, and wallet secret configuration. These are legitimate Hummingbot concepts, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Installer Commands Download and Execute External Software
medium_findings.description: The getting started guide includes shell examples that download Anaconda installers and run system package installation commands. These are normal installation steps, but users should verify sources before executing them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit download and install examples. They point to known installation sources, but they still ask users to execute external software and privileged package operations.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Matches Dismissed as False Positives
low_findings.description: The static prompt-injection findings match ordinary documentation text about what to do if a user forgets an exchange Secret Key. No instruction attempts to override the evaluator or model behavior were found at these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines use the word forget only in the phrase about forgetting a Secret Key. They contain no system-style instruction, role override, or request to change review behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The many hardcoded URL findings are primarily links to Hummingbot, exchange, installer, and API documentation. They are expected in reference documentation but should still be treated as external destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected examples show documentation and installer URLs rather than exfiltration endpoints. The conclusion is high confidence for the sampled high-signal locations, not every URL in the corpus.

dangerous_patterns.title: World-Writable Docker Socket Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The phrase sudo chmod 666 /var/run/docker.sock is a dangerous administrative pattern because Docker socket access can allow host compromise.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and target file are unambiguous. This is dangerous even when presented as troubleshooting guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Privileged Removal Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The command sudo rm -rf .password_verification is a destructive privileged shell pattern tied to password reset and credential storage documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is exact and repeated. It is scoped to a local Hummingbot file, which reduces confidence of maliciousness but not operational danger.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wallet Secret Configuration Field
dangerous_patterns.description: The connector reference includes an xrpl_secret_key configuration field with a long encoded value. Users could accidentally expose wallet credentials while seeking help.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The field name explicitly denotes a secret key. The value appears to be an example encrypted blob, so this is a credential-handling risk rather than confirmed leakage.

content.value_statement: Crypto trading bots require careful setup across exchanges, strategies, and secrets. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code answer Hummingbot configuration, connector, strategy, and troubleshooting questions from bundled documentation.
actual_capabilities: Explains Hummingbot installation, configuration, and startup workflows from the included references.
Guides users through exchange connector concepts, API key setup, and connector troubleshooting.
Summarizes market making, arbitrage, and strategy development documentation.
Helps interpret Gateway, DEX connector, wallet, and network setup references.
Provides targeted troubleshooting steps for common Hummingbot errors and environment issues.
limitations: Does not execute trades or connect to exchanges by itself.
Does not validate live exchange API changes or market conditions.
Includes commands that require human review before execution.
Can explain credential setup, but users must not paste real secrets into prompts.

use_cases.title: Set up a first trading bot
use_cases.description: Use the getting started references to understand installation, launch steps, password setup, and the first connector workflow.

use_cases.title: Debug exchange connector issues
use_cases.description: Find relevant connector notes, API key requirements, unsupported order behavior, and troubleshooting guidance for exchange integrations.

use_cases.title: Plan strategy development
use_cases.description: Use strategy and development references to compare market making, arbitrage, data feeds, and custom strategy implementation patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Find the right setup path
prompt_templates.prompt: I am new to Hummingbot. Explain the safest setup path for my operating system and list commands I should review before running.

prompt_templates.title: Configure an exchange connector
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure a Hummingbot connector for [exchange]. Explain the required API permissions and what secrets I should never share.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot a startup error
prompt_templates.prompt: Hummingbot fails with this error: [paste error without secrets]. Use the bundled troubleshooting references to identify likely causes and safe next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review a strategy plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Hummingbot strategy plan for [market pair]. Identify configuration gaps, exchange connector constraints, and risk controls to verify before live trading.

output_examples.input: How should I troubleshoot Docker permission errors for Hummingbot?
output_examples.output: The skill explains the documented Docker socket error, warns that broad socket permissions are risky, and suggests safer verification steps before changing system permissions.

output_examples.input: What do I need before connecting an exchange account?
output_examples.output: The skill summarizes API key creation, required permissions, withdrawal restrictions, password encryption, and reminders not to expose live keys in chat.

output_examples.input: Which strategy reference should I read for market making?
output_examples.output: The skill points to the strategy documentation, summarizes market making concepts, and lists configuration areas to inspect before using real funds.

best_practices: Use read-only or trade-only exchange keys and disable withdrawals whenever possible.
Review every shell command before execution, especially sudo, Docker, curl, and rm commands.
Test strategies with paper trading or small limits before using production balances.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API secrets, wallet keys, seed phrases, or passwords into prompts.
Do not run privileged commands from documentation without understanding the local impact.
Do not assume bundled exchange instructions match current exchange interfaces or policies.

faq.question: Can this skill trade for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation guidance only. It does not place orders or connect to exchanges.

faq.question: Is it safe to paste API keys for debugging?
faq.answer: No. Replace API keys, secrets, wallet keys, and account identifiers with placeholders before asking for help.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The references include privileged commands, Docker socket permission changes, and credential reset guidance that can harm a system if misused.

faq.question: Does the skill include official Hummingbot documentation?
faq.answer: The skill states that it was generated from official Hummingbot documentation and stores the material in reference files.

faq.question: Can it help with connector setup?
faq.answer: Yes. It can explain connector requirements, API permission concepts, and related troubleshooting notes from the bundled references.

faq.question: Should I verify commands outside the skill?
faq.answer: Yes. Verify current Hummingbot and exchange documentation before running commands or changing account permissions.
